chunks.text: CSC 675/775 Introduction to Database Systems Instructor: Joseph Hui Email: jhui@sfsu.edu What are databases and why are they important? IEEE Top Programming Languages • Graphs from googling “popular programming languages” • IEEE stands for “Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers” • The “Spectrum” ranking is weighted towards the profile of the typical IEEE member • The “Trending” ranking seeks to spot languages that are in the zeitgeist • The “Jobs” ranking measures what employers are looking for. Programming Languages by Spectrum Programming Languages by Trending Programming Languages by Jobs Database Management System (DBMS) • Storage and retrieval of data • Manage collections of interrelated structured data • Multiuser access • Transaction Management • Recovery in case of crash Relational Model • Collection of tables describing data • Everything is stored in tables (rows and columns like spreadsheets) • Relations, Relationships, semantics, constraints • Each table has columns defined • Structured data • Datatypes predefined and apply to all data in the column • Rows are instances of data stored in the table • Conceptual model • Entity Relationship Diagram (ERD) • Visual description of tables in a database Relational Model ID name dept_name budget 39827 John Physics 85000 48836 Kim Comp. Sci 50000 29987 Califieri Biology 90000 56655 Katz Physics 85000 Relational Model ID name dept_id 398227 John 1 48836 Kim 2 29987 Califeri 3 56655 Katz 1 dep_id dept_name budget 1 Physics 85000 2 Comp. Sci. 50000 3 Biology 90000 (a) Instructor table (b) Department table Data Definition Language - DDL Example DDL definition of tables instructor and department (MySQL) Data Manipulation Language - DML • Query language • Complete • Selection, Projection, Cartesian product, Union, Intersection, Set difference • Industry standard is Structured Query Language (SQL) • SQL is the most widely used data access language • SQL queries, Triggers, Procedures, Functions SELECT * FROM INSTRUCTOR WHERE DEPT_ID = 1; ID name dept_id 398227 John 1 56655 Katz 1 UPDATE INSTRUCTOR SET NAME = ‘Nina’ WHERE ID = 56655; ID name dept_id 398227 John 1 48836 Kim 2 29987 Califeri 3 56655 Nina 1 Data Manipulation Language - DML Database Design • Enforces integrity and relationship in tables through its schema • Entity Relationship model • Entity Relationship Diagram • Like UML Class Diagram • Normalization • Reduce redundancy • Optimize storage and access Database Design – ERD Representation RelationShip “Works In” Attribute Dept_id Attribute ID Attribute Name Attribute Name Attribute Budget Attribute Dept_id Entity “Instructor” Entity “Department” Relational Model: Data Models (EERD) Table of my favorite albums • Attribute may not contain a set of values or nested record Album ID Catalog Number Album Title Artist Released Singles Label Publishe r Country Publisher Website 101 PE 34188 Boston - debut Boston More Than a Feeling Peace of Mind Long Time Epic US www.epic.com 102 CBS 80959 Born to Run Bruce Springstee n Thunder Road Born to Run She’s the One Colum bia US www.columbia. com 103 PCS-7096 Let It Be Beatles Let it Be The Long and Winding Road Get Back Elektra US www.Elektra.com 104 CAQ 33280 That’s the Way of the World Earth, Wind & Fire Shining Star That’s the Way of the World Reasons Colum bia US www.columbia. com 105 2GHS 2031 Cats Andrew Mr Mistoffelees Geffen UK www geffen com 1st Normal Form (1NF)

debut Boston Epic US www.epic.com 102 CBS 80959 Born to Run Bruce Springsteen Columbi a US www.columbia. com • Pulled the multi-value attribute, Released Singles, into a separate table 2nd Normal Form (2NF) • Every non-key column must depend on the whole key, not just part of it Album ID Released Singles 101 More Than a Feeling 101 Peace of Mind 101 Long Time 102 Thunder Road 102 Born to Run 102 She’s the One Album Single Album ID Catalog Number Album Title Artist Label Publishe r Country Publisher Website 101 PE 34188 Boston - debut Boston Epic US www.epic.com 102 CBS 80959 Born to Run Bruce Springsteen Columbi a US www.columbia. com • Album ID is the Primary Key • Not a composite key, so by definition, it’s 2NF Single ID Album ID Released Singles 201 101 More Than a Feeling 203 101 Peace of Mind 206 101 Long Time 401 102 Thunder Road 408 102 Born to Run 409 102 She’s the One Album Single Album ID Catalog Number Album Title Artist Publishe r ID 101 PE 34188 Boston - debut Boston 601 102 CBS 80959 Born to Run Bruce Springsteen 602 3rd Normal Form (3NF) • Column values cannot depend on other columns’ values in the table Publish er ID Label Publisher Country Publisher Website 601 Epic US www.epic.com 602 Columbi a US www.columbia. com 603 Elektra US www.Elektra.com 701 Geffen UK www.geffen.com Publisher • Pulled all three Publisher columns into a separate table because the Publisher data depends on the Label value Database Optimization • Table design • High updates on data should have many tables with few columns • High reads on data should have fewer tables with many columns • Index definitions • Balanced Trees (B-trees) • Searchable and join columns indexed Typical use cases for RDBMS • Structured data sets • User information • Bank account information • Need to guarantee data consistency • Bank account balance • Even after a system crash • Searches for strings like <searchString> or <searchString%> • Wildcards at the end search strings (or none) • Online Transaction Processing • 32K concurrent connections Tough use cases for RDBMS • Multimedia • Streaming audio or video files • Genomic data (DNA) • 3 Billion base pairs composed of 4 letters (ACTG) • Searches for strings like %searchString% • Wildcards at the beginning of search strings • Distributed number of high reads • Billions of users reading billions of posts (social

Searches for strings like <searchString> or <searchString%> • Wildcards at the end search strings (or none) • Online Transaction Processing • 32K concurrent connections Tough use cases for RDBMS • Multimedia • Streaming audio or video files • Genomic data (DNA) • 3 Billion base pairs composed of 4 letters (ACTG) • Searches for strings like %searchString% • Wildcards at the beginning of search strings • Distributed number of high reads • Billions of users reading billions of posts (social media) Java and Relational Databases • JDBC • Java DataBase Connectivity • Typical JDBC interaction • Each row in a table becomes an instance of a class • i.e., each row in Instructor table becomes an instance of Instructor.java • The 3 main objects in JDBC • Connection • Statement • ResultSet • Object Relational Mapper (ORM)
